title: CI/CD with GitHub Actions
description: Automate your entire development pipeline -- from linting and testing to building Docker images and deploying to production -- using GitHub Actions workflows.
keyPoints: CI (Continuous Integration) automatically runs linters and tests on every push so broken code never reaches the main branch
CD (Continuous Deployment) automatically deploys your application when code is merged to main
Workflow files live in .github/workflows/ and use YAML syntax to define triggers, jobs, and steps
Matrix builds test your code across multiple versions of Node.js or Python simultaneously
Caching dependencies (node_modules, pip) can reduce CI run times by 50% or more
Branch protection rules enforce that CI must pass before a pull request can be merged

blocks.heading: What is CI/CD?
blocks.body: Imagine you are working on a team building a house. Without a system, every contractor just does their own thing -- the plumber installs pipes that do not align with the electrician's wiring, and nobody notices until the walls are sealed. CI/CD is like having an inspector check every contractor's work before it goes into the house.

**Continuous Integration (CI)** means every time a developer pushes code, an automated system immediately runs linters, tests, and builds to verify the code works. If anything fails, the developer gets feedback in minutes, not days.

**Continuous Deployment (CD)** takes it one step further. Once the code passes all checks, it is automatically deployed to production. No manual steps, no waiting for someone to press a button on a Friday afternoon.

cards.title: Catch Bugs Early
cards.body: Tests run on every push. A bug introduced in a morning commit is caught before lunch, not discovered in production next week.

cards.title: Consistent Quality
cards.body: Every pull request goes through the same checks. No more "it works on my machine" because CI runs in a clean environment.

cards.title: Ship Faster
cards.body: Automated deployments mean code goes from merge to production in minutes. No more release day anxiety.

blocks.heading: Set Up CI/CD from Scratch
blocks.body: Let us walk through adding a complete CI/CD pipeline to an existing project. By the end of these steps, every push will be automatically linted, tested, and deployed.

blocks.title: Create the workflow directory
blocks.body: GitHub Actions looks for workflow files in a specific location. Create the required directory structure in your repository root.
blocks.code: mkdir -p .github/workflows

blocks.title: Write your first workflow file
blocks.body: Create a YAML file in the workflows directory. The filename can be anything (e.g., ci.yml). Start with a simple lint-and-test workflow that runs on every push and pull request. Use the example in the "Workflow File Anatomy" section below as your starting point.
blocks.code: touch .github/workflows/ci.yml

blocks.title: Add secrets and environment variables
blocks.body: Go to your repository on GitHub, then Settings > Secrets and variables > Actions. Add any secrets your workflow needs (deploy tokens, API keys, etc.). These will be available as `${{ secrets.SECRET_NAME }}` in your workflow.

blocks.title: Push and verify
blocks.body: Commit and push your workflow file. Go to the Actions tab on GitHub to see your workflow running. If it fails, click into the failed job to see the logs for each step. Fix any issues and push again -- the workflow will re-run automatically.
blocks.code:
git add .github/workflows/ci.yml
git commit -m "Add CI workflow"
git push

blocks.heading: Workflow File Anatomy
blocks.body: A GitHub Actions workflow is a YAML file that lives in the `.github/workflows/` directory. Every workflow has three main sections: a **name**, **triggers** (when it runs), and **jobs** (what it does). Let us break down each piece.

blocks.description: A minimal workflow that shows the basic structure. This runs on every push to main and every pull request.
blocks.code:
# Every workflow starts with a name
name: CI

# Triggers: when should this workflow run?
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]          # Run when code is pushed to main
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]          # Run when a PR targets main

# Jobs: what should the workflow do?
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest    # Use a fresh Ubuntu VM

    steps:
      # Step 1: Check out the code
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Step 2: Set up the runtime
      - name: Set up Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'

      # Step 3: Install dependencies
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      # Step 4: Run your checks
      - name: Lint
        run: npm run lint

      - name: Test
        run: npm test

      - name: Build
        run: npm run build

blocks.heading: Workflow YAML Keywords
rows: Display name for the workflow in GitHub UI
Defines what events trigger the workflow
Groups of steps that run on a fresh VM
Operating system for the job's VM
Individual commands or actions within a job
Reference a reusable action from the marketplace
Pass inputs to a reusable action
Declare job dependencies (run after another job)
Conditional execution of a step or job

blocks.heading: Trigger Types
blocks.body: GitHub Actions supports many trigger types beyond push and pull_request. Here are the most useful ones for a typical project.

blocks.description: Examples of different trigger configurations -- pick the ones that match your workflow.
blocks.code:
# Run on push to specific branches
on:
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
    paths:                       # Only run when these files change
      - 'frontend/**'
      - 'package.json'

---
# Run on pull request events
on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened]
    branches: [main]

---
# Run on a schedule (cron syntax, UTC time)
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 9 * * 1'          # Every Monday at 9 AM UTC

---
# Manual trigger with inputs
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      environment:
        description: 'Deploy target'
        required: true
        default: 'staging'
        type: choice
        options:
          - staging
          - production

blocks.heading: Workflow Triggers Reference
rows: When commits are pushed to a branch
Run CI on every push to main or develop; use paths filter to only run when relevant files change
When a PR is opened, updated, or reopened
Run linters and tests on every PR before merging; the most common CI trigger
Nightly builds, weekly dependency audits, scheduled security scans (e.g., cron: '0 2 * * *' for 2 AM daily)
Manually triggered from the GitHub UI
On-demand deployments, manual database migrations, one-off tasks that need human approval
When a GitHub release is published
Build and publish Docker images, npm packages, or binaries when you tag a release
Create reusable workflows that multiple repos or pipelines can share
Triggered by an external webhook (API call)
Start a workflow from an external service like Slack, a CMS, or another CI system

blocks.heading: Environment Variables and Secrets
blocks.body: Workflows often need sensitive values like API keys, database passwords, or deploy tokens. Never put these in your YAML file. Instead, store them as GitHub Secrets (Settings > Secrets and variables > Actions) and reference them with the `secrets` context.

blocks.description: How to use environment variables and secrets in your workflow steps.
blocks.code:
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    # Job-level environment variables (available to all steps)
    env:
      NODE_ENV: production
      NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL: https://api.example.com

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Build with env vars
        run: npm run build
        env:
          # Step-level env var (overrides job-level)
          BUILD_ID: ${{ github.sha }}

      - name: Deploy to server
        run: |
          echo "Deploying commit ${{ github.sha }}"
          ./deploy.sh
        env:
          # Secrets are masked in logs (shown as ***)
          DEPLOY_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_TOKEN }}
          DATABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.DATABASE_URL }}

blocks.title: Never hardcode secrets in YAML files
blocks.body: Your workflow files are committed to git and visible to anyone with repo access. Always use GitHub Secrets for passwords, tokens, and API keys. Secrets are encrypted at rest and masked in log output.

blocks.title: Secrets are not available in fork PRs
blocks.body: For security, GitHub does not pass repository secrets to workflows triggered by pull requests from forks. This prevents a malicious fork from stealing your secrets. If your CI needs secrets to run tests, consider using the pull_request_target trigger with caution, or design tests so they can run without secrets.

blocks.heading: Matrix Builds
blocks.body: Matrix builds let you run the same job across multiple configurations simultaneously. This is how you test that your code works on Node 18, 20, and 22, or on both Ubuntu and macOS, without writing separate jobs.

blocks.description: A matrix build that tests across multiple Node.js versions in parallel.
blocks.code:
name: Matrix Test

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    strategy:
      matrix:
        node-version: [18, 20, 22]
      fail-fast: false       # Continue other matrix jobs even if one fails

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Node.js ${{ matrix.node-version }}
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }}

      - name: Install and test
        run: |
          npm ci
          npm run lint
          npm test

blocks.heading: Caching Dependencies
blocks.body: Installing dependencies from scratch on every CI run wastes time and bandwidth. Caching stores your node_modules or pip packages between runs so they only need to be downloaded when package.json or requirements.txt changes.

blocks.description: Caching both npm and pip dependencies to dramatically speed up CI runs.
blocks.code:
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Node.js with built-in cache support
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'
          cache: 'npm'                               # Built-in npm cache
          cache-dependency-path: frontend/package-lock.json

      # Python with manual cache configuration
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - name: Cache pip dependencies
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.cache/pip
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-pip-${{ hashFiles('backend/requirements.txt') }}
          restore-keys: |
            ${{ runner.os }}-pip-

      - name: Install frontend
        run: cd frontend && npm ci            # Cache hit = ~5s instead of ~30s

      - name: Install backend
        run: cd backend && pip install -r requirements.txt

blocks.heading: Complete Lint and Test Workflow
blocks.body: Here is a production-ready workflow that lints and tests both the frontend and backend. This is the workflow that runs on every pull request to prevent broken code from being merged.

blocks.description: A complete lint-and-test workflow for a full-stack project with both frontend (Next.js) and backend (Flask).
blocks.code:
name: Lint & Test

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  frontend:
    name: Frontend Checks
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'
          cache: 'npm'
          cache-dependency-path: frontend/package-lock.json

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci
        working-directory: frontend

      - name: Run ESLint
        run: npm run lint
        working-directory: frontend

      - name: Run TypeScript type check
        run: npx tsc --noEmit
        working-directory: frontend

      - name: Run Jest tests with coverage
        run: npm test -- --coverage --ci
        working-directory: frontend

      - name: Upload coverage report
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: frontend-coverage
          path: frontend/coverage/
          retention-days: 7

  backend:
    name: Backend Checks
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - name: Cache pip
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.cache/pip
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-pip-${{ hashFiles('backend/requirements.txt') }}

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt
        working-directory: backend

      - name: Run flake8 linting
        run: |
          pip install flake8
          flake8 . --max-line-length=120 --exclude=__pycache__
        working-directory: backend

      - name: Run pytest
        run: |
          pip install pytest
          pytest -v --tb=short
        working-directory: backend

      - name: Seed database (verify seed data is valid)
        run: |
          FLASK_APP=app:create_app python -m flask seed-db
        working-directory: backend

blocks.heading: Deploy to Vercel Workflow
blocks.body: Vercel is one of the most popular hosting platforms for Next.js applications. While Vercel has its own built-in CI/CD, using GitHub Actions gives you more control -- you can run your own tests first, deploy to preview environments on PRs, and only deploy to production after all checks pass.

blocks.description: Deploy to Vercel with preview deployments on PRs and production deployments on merge to main.
blocks.code:
name: Deploy to Vercel

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    name: Lint & Test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'
          cache: 'npm'
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run lint
      - run: npm test

  deploy-preview:
    name: Deploy Preview
    needs: test
    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: preview
      url: ${{ steps.deploy.outputs.url }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install Vercel CLI
        run: npm install -g vercel

      - name: Pull Vercel environment
        run: vercel pull --yes --environment=preview --token=${{ secrets.VERCEL_TOKEN }}

      - name: Build
        run: vercel build --token=${{ secrets.VERCEL_TOKEN }}

      - name: Deploy to preview
        id: deploy
        run: |
          URL=$(vercel deploy --prebuilt --token=${{ secrets.VERCEL_TOKEN }})
          echo "url=$URL" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

  deploy-production:
    name: Deploy Production
    needs: test
    if: github.event_name == 'push' && github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: production
      url: https://myapp.vercel.app
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install Vercel CLI
        run: npm install -g vercel

      - name: Pull Vercel environment
        run: vercel pull --yes --environment=production --token=${{ secrets.VERCEL_TOKEN }}

      - name: Build
        run: vercel build --prod --token=${{ secrets.VERCEL_TOKEN }}

      - name: Deploy to production
        run: vercel deploy --prebuilt --prod --token=${{ secrets.VERCEL_TOKEN }}

blocks.heading: Release Workflow with Changelog
blocks.body: When you tag a release (e.g., v1.2.0), this workflow automatically generates a changelog from your commit history and creates a GitHub Release. It is a great way to keep users informed about what changed in each version.

blocks.description: Automatically create GitHub releases with changelogs when you push a version tag.
blocks.code:
name: Release

on:
  push:
    tags:
      - 'v*'                     # Trigger on version tags like v1.0.0, v2.1.3

permissions:
  contents: write                # Needed to create releases

jobs:
  release:
    name: Create Release
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0           # Fetch full history for changelog generation

      - name: Set up Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'
          cache: 'npm'

      - name: Install and build
        run: |
          npm ci
          npm run build

      - name: Run tests
        run: npm test

      - name: Generate changelog
        id: changelog
        run: |
          # Get commits since the previous tag
          PREV_TAG=$(git describe --tags --abbrev=0 HEAD^ 2>/dev/null || echo "")
          if [ -z "$PREV_TAG" ]; then
            CHANGES=$(git log --pretty=format:"- %s (%h)" HEAD)
          else
            CHANGES=$(git log --pretty=format:"- %s (%h)" $PREV_TAG..HEAD)
          fi
          # Write to output (multiline)
          echo "changes<<EOF" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          echo "$CHANGES" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          echo "EOF" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

      - name: Create GitHub Release
        uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v2
        with:
          body: |
            ## What's Changed
            ${{ steps.changelog.outputs.changes }}
          draft: false
          prerelease: ${{ contains(github.ref, '-rc') || contains(github.ref, '-beta') }}

blocks.heading: Branch Protection Workflow
blocks.body: You can enforce branch protection programmatically using a workflow that checks PR metadata -- such as requiring a specific title format, labels, or linked issues. This complements the GitHub branch protection UI settings.

blocks.description: Enforce PR conventions: title format, required labels, and linked issues.
blocks.code:
name: PR Checks

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, edited, labeled, unlabeled, synchronize]

jobs:
  pr-lint:
    name: Validate PR
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - name: Check PR title format
        run: |
          TITLE="${{ github.event.pull_request.title }}"
          # Require conventional commit format: type(scope): description
          if ! echo "$TITLE" | grep -qE '^(feat|fix|docs|style|refactor|test|chore)(\(.+\))?: .+'; then
            echo "::error::PR title must follow conventional commit format."
            echo "Examples: 'feat(auth): add login page', 'fix: resolve crash on startup'"
            exit 1
          fi

      - name: Check for required labels
        run: |
          LABELS='${{ toJSON(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name) }}'
          # Require at least one type label
          if ! echo "$LABELS" | grep -qE '(bug|feature|enhancement|documentation|chore)'; then
            echo "::error::PR must have at least one type label (bug, feature, enhancement, documentation, or chore)."
            exit 1
          fi

blocks.heading: Build and Deploy Workflow
blocks.body: This workflow runs after the lint-and-test workflow passes. It builds the production application and deploys it. The `needs` keyword ensures it only runs if CI succeeds.

blocks.description: A deployment workflow that builds the frontend, packages the backend, and deploys to a server. Only runs on pushes to main after CI passes.
blocks.code:
name: Build & Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  # Run tests first
  test:
    uses: ./.github/workflows/lint-and-test.yml

  # Build only if tests pass
  build:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      image_tag: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'
          cache: 'npm'
          cache-dependency-path: frontend/package-lock.json

      - name: Build frontend
        run: |
          cd frontend
          npm ci
          npm run build

      - name: Upload build artifacts
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: frontend-build
          path: frontend/.next/
          retention-days: 1

  # Deploy only if build passes
  deploy:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production     # Requires manual approval if configured

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Download build artifacts
        uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: frontend-build
          path: frontend/.next/

      - name: Deploy to server
        run: |
          echo "Deploying to production..."
          # Example: rsync to a server
          # rsync -avz --delete frontend/.next/ user@server:/app/frontend/.next/
          # Or: deploy with Docker (see Docker workflow below)
        env:
          DEPLOY_KEY: ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_KEY }}

blocks.heading: Docker Build and Publish Workflow
blocks.body: If your application runs in Docker containers, you need a workflow that builds the Docker images, tags them, and pushes them to a container registry like GitHub Container Registry (GHCR). A full-stack project has multiple images (frontend, backend), so the workflow should build **all** of them consistently. Using a matrix strategy keeps the workflow DRY, and adding QEMU enables multi-platform builds for both `amd64` (standard servers) and `arm64` (AWS Graviton, Apple Silicon, Raspberry Pi).

blocks.description: A complete Docker workflow that builds multi-platform images for both frontend and backend, and pushes them to GitHub Container Registry with distinctive prefixed names.
blocks.code:
name: Docker Build & Publish

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    tags: ['v*']               # Also run on version tags like v1.0.0

jobs:
  docker-push:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write          # Required to push to GHCR

    # Matrix strategy builds both images with the same steps.
    # No build-args needed -- the frontend Dockerfile bakes a placeholder,
    # and docker-entrypoint.sh seds in the real URL at container start.
    strategy:
      matrix:
        include:
          - image: myapp-backend
            context: ./backend
          - image: myapp-frontend
            context: ./frontend

    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # QEMU enables cross-platform builds (arm64 on an amd64 runner)
      - name: Set up QEMU
        uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v3

      - name: Set up Docker Buildx
        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Log in to GitHub Container Registry
        uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Extract metadata (tags, labels)
        id: meta
        uses: docker/metadata-action@v5
        with:
          images: ghcr.io/${{ github.repository_owner }}/${{ matrix.image }}
          tags: |
            type=sha
            type=raw,value=latest

      - name: Build and push image
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with:
          context: ${{ matrix.context }}
          push: true
          platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64
          tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
          labels: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.labels }}
          cache-from: type=gha
          cache-to: type=gha,mode=max

blocks.title: Why prefix image names?
blocks.body: Without a prefix, images like `frontend` and `backend` are generic and easy to confuse with other projects. Prefixing with your project name (e.g., `myapp-frontend`, `myapp-backend`) makes them immediately identifiable in your container registry.

blocks.heading: Multi-Platform Build Components
rows: Lets an amd64 GitHub runner build arm64 images
Required for multi-platform builds and advanced caching
Produces a single manifest that works on Intel/AMD and ARM servers
Uses GitHub Actions cache for Docker layers
Speeds up subsequent builds by reusing unchanged layers
Runtime sed script in the frontend image
Replaces a build-time placeholder with the real NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL at container start -- one image works in any environment

blocks.heading: GitHub Actions vs Other CI/CD Platforms
blocks.body: GitHub Actions is not the only CI/CD platform. Here is how it compares to two other popular options. The best choice depends on where your code lives and what features matter most to your team.
**Best for:** Projects already hosted on GitHub. Tightly integrated with pull requests, issues, and the GitHub ecosystem.

blocks.heading: Strengths
items: Zero setup -- workflows live in your repo and run automatically
Massive marketplace of reusable actions (20,000+)
Free for public repos, generous free tier for private repos (2,000 minutes/month)
Native integration with GitHub features (status checks, environments, deployments)
Matrix builds, caching, and artifacts built in

blocks.heading: Limitations
items: Tied to GitHub -- cannot use with GitLab or Bitbucket repos
YAML-only configuration (no visual pipeline editor)
Debugging can be tedious (no SSH into runners on the free tier)

blocks.body: **Best for:** Teams using GitLab for source control, or organizations that want a self-hosted, all-in-one DevOps platform.

blocks.heading: Strengths
items: Built-in container registry, package registry, and security scanning
Visual pipeline editor and pipeline graphs
Easy to self-host runners on your own infrastructure
Powerful environment and approval workflows
Auto DevOps can generate CI/CD pipelines automatically

blocks.heading: Limitations
items: Configuration file (.gitlab-ci.yml) can become very complex for large projects
Smaller ecosystem of reusable components compared to GitHub Actions marketplace
Free tier includes only 400 CI/CD minutes per month

blocks.body: **Best for:** Teams that need advanced features like SSH debugging, Docker layer caching, and granular resource control.

blocks.heading: Strengths
items: SSH into running CI jobs for live debugging
Excellent Docker support with built-in Docker layer caching
Orbs (reusable config packages) simplify complex setups
Works with GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket
Fine-grained resource classes (choose CPU and RAM per job)

blocks.heading: Limitations
items: External service -- requires granting third-party access to your repo
Free tier limited to 6,000 build minutes per month but only 1 concurrent job
Configuration syntax has a steeper learning curve than GitHub Actions

blocks.heading: Branch Protection Rules
blocks.body: Workflows are only useful if they are enforced. Branch protection rules prevent anyone from pushing directly to main or merging a pull request until all CI checks pass.

blocks.heading: Setting Up Branch Protection
items: Go to your repository on GitHub and click Settings
Click Branches in the left sidebar
Click Add branch protection rule
Enter main as the branch name pattern
Enable Require a pull request before merging
Enable Require status checks to pass before merging
Search for and select your CI job names (e.g., Frontend Checks, Backend Checks)
Enable Require branches to be up to date before merging
Click Create to save the rule

blocks.title: Why require up-to-date branches?
blocks.body: Without this setting, a PR could pass CI, then another PR gets merged to main, and the first PR's code might conflict with the new changes. Requiring up-to-date branches ensures CI runs against the latest main before merging.

blocks.heading: Reusable Workflows
blocks.body: As your project grows, you will have similar CI logic across multiple workflows. Reusable workflows let you define a workflow once and call it from other workflows, just like functions in code.

blocks.description: A reusable workflow that any other workflow can call to set up Node.js and run tests.
blocks.code:
# This workflow can be called by other workflows
name: Reusable Node.js Test

on:
  workflow_call:                 # This makes it callable
    inputs:
      node-version:
        required: false
        type: string
        default: '22'
      working-directory:
        required: false
        type: string
        default: 'frontend'

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ inputs.node-version }}
          cache: 'npm'
          cache-dependency-path: ${{ inputs.working-directory }}/package-lock.json

      - run: npm ci
        working-directory: ${{ inputs.working-directory }}

      - run: npm run lint
        working-directory: ${{ inputs.working-directory }}

      - run: npm test -- --ci
        working-directory: ${{ inputs.working-directory }}

blocks.description: A workflow that calls the reusable workflow above, keeping the main workflow file clean and simple.
blocks.code:
name: Main Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  frontend-test:
    uses: ./.github/workflows/reusable-node-test.yml
    with:
      node-version: '22'
      working-directory: 'frontend'

  deploy:
    needs: frontend-test
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: echo "Deploying to production..."

blocks.heading: Artifact Uploads
blocks.body: Sometimes you need to save files from a CI run -- coverage reports, build outputs, test screenshots. Artifacts let you upload files during a workflow and download them later from the GitHub UI or in subsequent jobs.

blocks.description: Uploading test results and build artifacts so they can be downloaded from the GitHub Actions UI.
blocks.code:
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'

      - run: npm ci && npm test -- --coverage
        working-directory: frontend

      # Upload coverage report (always, even if tests fail)
      - name: Upload coverage
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: always()
        with:
          name: coverage-report
          path: frontend/coverage/
          retention-days: 14

      # Upload Playwright screenshots on failure
      - name: Upload test screenshots
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: failure()
        with:
          name: test-screenshots
          path: frontend/test-results/
          retention-days: 7

blocks.heading: Workflow Recipes
blocks.body: Here are ready-to-use patterns for common CI/CD tasks. Expand any recipe to see the implementation.

blocks.title: Auto-label PRs based on changed files

blocks.body: Automatically add labels like `frontend`, `backend`, or `docs` to pull requests based on which files were changed. This makes it easy to filter and review PRs.

blocks.code:
name: Auto Label PR

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize]

jobs:
  label:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      pull-requests: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/labeler@v5
        with:
          repo-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

blocks.body: Create a `.github/labeler.yml` file to define the mapping: `frontend: ['frontend/**']`, `backend: ['backend/**']`, etc.
Run browser-based end-to-end tests in CI using Playwright. This spins up your app in a container and tests it with real browsers.

blocks.code:
name: E2E Tests

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  e2e:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'
      - run: npm ci
        working-directory: frontend
      - name: Install Playwright browsers
        run: npx playwright install --with-deps
        working-directory: frontend
      - name: Run Playwright tests
        run: npx playwright test
        working-directory: frontend
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: failure()
        with:
          name: playwright-report
          path: frontend/playwright-report/

blocks.body: Run `npm audit` on a weekly schedule to catch known vulnerabilities in your dependencies before they become a problem.

blocks.code:
name: Security Audit

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 9 * * 1'    # Every Monday at 9 AM UTC
  workflow_dispatch:         # Allow manual trigger

jobs:
  audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'
      - run: npm ci
        working-directory: frontend
      - name: Run security audit
        run: npm audit --audit-level=high
        working-directory: frontend
        continue-on-error: true
      - name: Check for critical vulnerabilities
        run: npm audit --audit-level=critical
        working-directory: frontend

blocks.body: Automatically comment on pull requests with the bundle size of the build, so reviewers can catch unexpected size increases.

blocks.code:
name: Bundle Size

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  size:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      pull-requests: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'
          cache: 'npm'
      - run: npm ci && npm run build
        working-directory: frontend
      - name: Calculate and comment bundle size
        run: |
          SIZE=$(du -sh frontend/.next/ | cut -f1)
          gh pr comment ${{ github.event.number }} \
            --body "Build size: **$SIZE** (commit ${{ github.sha }})"
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

blocks.body: Send a Slack notification when a deployment succeeds or fails, so the team knows immediately.
Fail the CI build if code coverage drops below a minimum threshold. This prevents merging PRs that reduce test coverage.

blocks.code:
- name: Run tests with coverage
        run: npm test -- --coverage --ci
        working-directory: frontend

      - name: Check coverage threshold
        run: |
          # Extract coverage percentage from the summary
          COVERAGE=$(npx istanbul report --include=frontend/coverage/coverage-final.json text-summary | grep 'Statements' | awk '{print $3}' | tr -d '%')
          echo "Coverage: $COVERAGE%"
          # Fail if coverage is below 80%
          if (( $(echo "$COVERAGE < 80" | bc -l) )); then
            echo "::error::Code coverage ($COVERAGE%) is below the 80% threshold."
            exit 1
          fi

blocks.heading: Common issues and how to fix them
items: Permission denied: Add the required permissions block to your job (e.g., packages: write for GHCR)
Cache miss every time: Make sure your cache key includes the hash of the lockfile, not the requirements file content
Tests pass locally but fail in CI: CI uses a clean environment -- check for missing environment variables or files
Workflow not triggering: Verify the branch name in your 'on' triggers matches exactly (main vs master)
npm ci fails: Ensure package-lock.json is committed to git and matches package.json
Docker build fails: Check that the Dockerfile path in context is correct relative to the repo root
Secrets not available: Secrets are not passed to workflows triggered by pull requests from forks (security measure)

blocks.heading: Security Tips for Secrets Management
blocks.body: Secrets are the most sensitive part of your CI/CD pipeline. A leaked deploy token or API key can compromise your entire production environment. Follow these rules to keep your pipeline secure.

blocks.title: Use environment-scoped secrets for production
blocks.body: GitHub lets you scope secrets to specific environments (e.g., production, staging). This means your production deploy token is only available to jobs that target the production environment, which you can gate behind required reviewers. Go to Settings > Environments > production > Add secret to set this up.

blocks.title: Rotate secrets regularly and use OIDC when possible
blocks.body: Static secrets (long-lived tokens) are risky because a single leak exposes them forever. Rotate deploy tokens and API keys at least every 90 days. For cloud providers (AWS, GCP, Azure), use OpenID Connect (OIDC) instead of static credentials. OIDC generates short-lived tokens for each workflow run, so there is nothing long-lived to steal. GitHub has built-in OIDC support -- see the docs for `aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials` with `role-to-assume`.

blocks.heading: GitHub Actions Cheat Sheet
rows: Run a step only on failure
Run steps in a specific directory

blocks.title: Your CI/CD pipeline is ready
blocks.body: You now have everything you need to set up automated linting, testing, building, and deployment with GitHub Actions. Start with the lint-and-test workflow on your next project, then add deployment workflows as your project matures. Remember: the best CI pipeline is one that your team actually trusts and uses.

references.title: Continuous Delivery: Reliable Software Releases through Build, Test, and Deployment Automation
